title: Conversion-Friction Cartography

content.statement: Conversion is the sum of micro-frictions.
content.question: If you mapped every step in your signup flow, where's the single highest-leverage friction to remove?
content.equation: CFC = Σ(Step Friction × Importance × Traffic Volume)
sop: Map every single step of your funnel from first click to purchase or signup completion, no matter how minor.
Pull step-by-step drop-off data, or run a 10-person user test, to see where people actually quit.
Rate each step's Friction (1-10) and Importance (1-10), and note Traffic Volume at that step (see calculation, page 5).
Rank steps by Friction times Importance times Traffic Volume, and fix the top 3 first.
Re-test conversion after each fix before moving to the next step, don't batch all the changes at once.
content.explainer: You don't fix conversion with one big change. You kill ten tiny frictions.
questions: If you mapped every step in your signup flow, where's the single highest-leverage friction to remove?
Which step in your funnel loses the most people relative to how little it actually accomplishes?
If you removed the top 3 friction points in your flow, would your conversion double, or is the real block somewhere else entirely?
content.equation_example: Step 1: Friction 3 times Importance 8 times Traffic 1,000 visitors equals 24,000. Step 2: Friction 8 times Importance 5 times Traffic 400 visitors equals 16,000. Sum equals 40,000, an index, not a percentage. Whichever step contributes the largest number to that total is your highest-leverage fix, attack it first, not the step that simply feels most annoying.
content.closing_statement: Small frictions rarely feel small in aggregate.

caption:
Principle Nº 95: Conversion-Friction Cartography

Conversion is the sum of micro-frictions.

Ask yourself: If you mapped every step in your signup flow, where's the single highest-leverage friction to remove?
